frameworks.name: One-Level-Up Channel Strategy
frameworks.definition: Instead of acquiring end customers directly, acquire the intermediary (agency, guru, mentor) who already has those customers, capturing higher LTV via onboarding fees, per-customer fees, and recurring top-line percentage.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that to meaningfully test a paid acquisition campaign, an operator must be willing to spend at least 2x their target CAC before drawing conclusions; at $2,000/month spend against a $1,500 CAC target, feedback frequency is so slow that a valid result takes roughly two months, making optimization impossible.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Paid ad campaign (Meta/Facebook/Instagram)
Target CAC known in advance
Operator expects to iterate on creative and targeting before declaring success
claims.mechanism: Below 2x CAC spend, the cadence of feedback (sales frequency) is so slow that you cannot gather enough signal to optimize; you may see one inefficient sale every two months, which provides no reliable data on whether the channel works.

numbers.axis: monthly ad spend as multiple of target CAC

anchors.value: feedback too slow to iterate; cannot know if channel works

anchors.region: at or above 2x target CAC (e.g., $3k+ against $1.5k CAC)
anchors.value: sufficient cadence to test and optimize campaign

bounded_by: Does not apply to organic channels where spend is near-zero
Assumes the operator is starting from scratch with a new campaign, not optimizing a proven one

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for a GoHighLevel reseller selling to online fitness coaches on Instagram, the highest-converting front-end hook is a set of five case studies featuring coaches with fewer than 5,000 followers who each achieved five new clients per week, because every prospect's first objection is whether the automation will work for their specific niche.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: SaaS or CRM product sold to Instagram-based fitness coaches
Prospect has objection: will this work for my specific avatar or follower count?
claims.mechanism: Showing five diverse representatives (different demographics, niches, follower counts) all succeeding with the same tool preemptively neutralizes the primary objection ('will this work for me?') before it is voiced, removing the main friction from the consideration stage.

numbers.axis: follower count of case study subjects

anchors.value: 5 clients per week achieved — used as intimidation-defying proof
less persuasive as proof because audience assumes large accounts succeed regardless

bounded_by: Assumes the product genuinely works for diverse fitness niches; fabricated case studies would violate this
Less relevant when selling to audiences that already have high social proof volume and do not feel their niche is uniquely challenging

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that going up one level to sell to fitness business coaches (intermediaries) rather than individual online fitness coaches dramatically increases LTV and unit economics, based on his parallel experience with a similar platform business: an agency could be acquired for roughly $5,000 in CAC, then paid $25,000 to onboard, with an additional $1,000 per customer (for a 50-customer agency, ~$50,000), plus 30% of top-line recurring revenue.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B SaaS or CRM platform with a recurring revenue model
Target intermediary already has 20-50+ end customers they serve
Operator is willing to manage two-sided customer success (intermediary + end customers)
claims.mechanism: Intermediaries aggregate demand from multiple end clients, so acquiring one intermediary yields revenue equivalent to dozens of direct sales; their recurring client relationship makes the intermediary's revenue stickier than individual coach subscriptions.
bounded_by: Requires building two-sided customer success capability; adds operational complexity
Does not apply if the intermediary does not already have an established client base paying them

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that selling client-acquisition tooling directly to individual fitness coaches who do not already have paying clients creates a downstream demand-generation problem: the coach will inevitably ask how to get leads, pulling the vendor into a full coaching/mentorship role they did not intend to fill.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: SaaS or automation platform sold to early-stage coaches (pre-10k/month revenue)
Platform's value prop is converting existing followers or traffic into customers
claims.mechanism: Coaches without established lead flow cannot use the product as designed; they turn to the vendor for lead generation help, which requires the vendor to provide an entirely different service (marketing mentorship), thereby creating support burden and blurring the product's scope.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the ideal customer profile is already-established coaches doing $10k+/month who have existing follower bases and need conversion tooling, not demand generation

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the correct messaging hook for a B2B SaaS or CRM tool targeting Instagram fitness coaches who already have followings is a variant of 'turn your Instagram followers into customers without DMing anyone,' because the primary pain is spending all day manually in the DMs.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Target audience is Instagram-based fitness coaches with existing followers
Product automates DM-based lead conversion
bounded_by: Does not apply to coaches who primarily use paid ads rather than organic Instagram to acquire customers
Less relevant for LinkedIn-focused coaches (estimated 20% or fewer of fitness coaches in this context)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the most persuasive channel partner hook for fitness business coaches (intermediaries) is a recurring-revenue framing: asking whether they are tired of giving clients the keys to the kingdom and watching them leave after three months, and contrasting this with the possibility of having every client they have ever sold still paying them years later.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Target is a fitness business coach or mentor whose current revenue model is one-time or short-term program sales
Platform offers a white-labeled recurring CRM or automation product the intermediary can resell to their client base
bounded_by: Less effective for intermediaries already running strong recurring membership models who do not feel the churn pain

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when running ad campaigns, the operator should identify the first working hook and then immediately increase spend ('crank'), rather than running multiple hooks simultaneously at low spend; one hook will work and the rest will not, and the winning hook should be concentrated on.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Multiple messaging hooks are being tested against the same target audience
Operator has limited budget and cannot fund all hooks at meaningful spend simultaneously

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for a GoHighLevel reseller, the ad creative strategy should lead with a seven-minute video showing exactly how followers can be converted to customers, followed by the five-case-study proof block, then a VSL, a sales call, and a close — and cash should be accelerated to the front of the transaction as much as possible.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: bingo bango, money mango type play
conditions: SaaS or CRM tool sold via inbound lead generation to fitness coaches
Offer has a recurring revenue component

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that going up one level to sell to business coaches who serve fitness coaches (rather than the fitness coaches themselves) results in stickier recurring revenue, because the intermediary (the business coach) has an institutional need to provide ongoing tooling to their clients and therefore churns far less than individual end-user coaches.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B SaaS sold through intermediary channel vs. sold direct to end user
Intermediary has its own ongoing client relationship with the end users
claims.mechanism: Business coaches who resell the platform to their clients need the platform as a delivery tool for their own coaching business; this creates institutional dependency rather than individual-preference dependency, making churn decisions organizationally costly rather than personally convenient.
bounded_by: Requires that the intermediary has a live client base they are actively serving; inactive or transactional intermediaries do not produce the same stickiness

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that a GoHighLevel reseller with a 7% monthly churn rate and a 4.6:1 LTV-to-CAC ratio should focus on scaling paid acquisition (Meta/Instagram ads) and strategic partnerships rather than further improving product retention, since unit economics are already sound at a $9,000 LTV and approximately $1,500 CAC.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Monthly churn 7% or below
LTV-to-CAC ratio at or above 4:1
Existing paid and partnership channels are profitable but at low spend
claims.mechanism: When unit economics (LTV:CAC) are already favorable, the binding constraint on revenue growth shifts from product quality to acquisition volume; further churn reduction has diminishing returns compared to unlocking more acquisition spend.
bounded_by: Does not apply if churn is above 15% monthly, in which case product improvement likely yields higher ROI than acquisition scaling

examples.text: Alex Hormozi described his experience building a parallel platform business with Alan that targeted brick-and-mortar fitness SMBs. Instead of selling directly to individual SMBs, he pivoted to selling to agencies (e.g., chiro agencies, fitness agencies) that already served those SMBs. The model worked as follows: acquire an agency for roughly $5,000 in CAC; the agency paid $25,000 to onboard onto the platform; each of the agency's ~50 clients paid $1,000 each ($50,000 total); and the ongoing recurring fee was 30% of the agency's top-line revenue. The agencies accepted because they received the back-end technical infrastructure they lacked, while Hormozi's platform captured compounding recurring revenue from every client the agency ever added. This model produced dramatically higher LTV than direct SMB acquisition and was used as a direct analogy for how the GoHighLevel reseller should approach fitness business coaches as the primary customer.

Alex Hormozi outlined a specific five-case-study creative format for a GoHighLevel CRM reseller targeting Instagram fitness coaches: find five coaches each with under 5,000 followers who achieved five new clients per week using the automation. Each case study should represent a different psychographic or avatar — e.g., a muscular white bodybuilder, a skinny older Black vegan, an Asian female vegan powerlifter, a mom over 50 — so that any prospect can find a representative who looks and trains like them. The function of the diversity is purely objection removal: every prospect's first internal question after seeing the ad is 'but will this work for my niche?' and the array of five case studies answers that before the question is asked.

practices.text: When selling a CRM or automation tool to Instagram fitness coaches, open the lead magnet video with a seven-minute walkthrough showing exactly how followers are converted to customers without manual DMing; brevity and specificity both reduce friction and raise perceived credibility.

For a GoHighLevel reseller seeking channel partners among fitness business coaches, test at least three distinct hooks (e.g., recurring revenue angle, churn prevention angle, compounding LTV angle) at sufficient spend before declaring which message resonates, then concentrate all spend on the winning hook.

When moving to an intermediary channel model (selling to gurus/agencies rather than end users), build two separate customer success functions: one managing the intermediary relationship and one managing the end-user experience, because the two sets of problems and escalations are structurally distinct.
